fields.note: Adding process_priority param to click.edit() and Editor.__init__ in _termui_impl.py; will also touch Editor.edit_file() to apply priority when launching subprocess.

content:
type: DRAFT
files: src/click/termui.py
region: edit() function
code: def edit(
    text: t.AnyStr | None = None,
    editor: str | None = None,
    env: cabc.Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
    require_save: bool = True,
    extension: str = ".txt",
    filename: str | cabc.Iterable[str] | None = None,
    process_priority: str | None = None,
) -> t.AnyStr | None:
    r"""Edits the given text in the defined editor.  If an editor is given
    (should be the full path to the executable but the regular operating
    system search path is used for finding the executable) it overrides
    the detected editor.  Optionally, some environment variables can be
    used.  If the editor is closed without changes, `None` is returned.  In
    case a file is edited directly the return value is always `None` and
    `require_save` and `extension` are ignored.

    If the editor cannot be opened a :exc:`UsageError` is raised.

    Note for Windows: to simplify cross-platform usage, the newlines are
    automatically converted from POSIX to Windows and vice versa.  As such,
    the message here will have ``\n`` as newline markers.

    :param text: the text to edit.
    :param editor: optionally the editor to use.  Defaults to automatic
                   detection.
    :param env: environment variables to forward to the editor.
    :param require_save: if this is true, then not saving in the editor
                         will make the return value become `None`.
    :param extension: the extension to tell the editor about.  This defaults
                      to `.txt` but changing this might change syntax
                      highlighting.
    :param filename: if provided it will edit this file instead of the
                     provided text contents.  It will not use a temporary
                     file as an indirection in that case. May be an
                     iterable of multiple filenames to edit them all at
                     once.
    :param process_priority: optionally set the CPU priority of the
                             editor process. One of ``'low'``,
                             ``'below_normal'``, ``'normal'``,
                             ``'above_normal'``, ``'high'``. Defaults to
                             ``None``, which leaves the priority
                             unchanged. Invalid values are ignored, and
                             failures to set priority do not cause the
                             edit operation to fail.
    """
    from ._termui_impl import Editor

    ed = Editor(
        editor=editor,
        env=env,
        require_save=require_save,
        extension=extension,
        process_priority=process_priority,
    )

    if filename is None:
        return ed.edit(text)

    if isinstance(filename, str):
        ed.edit_files([filename])
    else:
        ed.edit_files(filename)
    return None
note: Merged: your multi-file filename param + my process_priority param. Editor() call now passes process_priority kwarg (I own that field; your other kwargs unchanged). filename handling / edit_files call is your logic.

    text: t.AnyStr | None = None,

    env: cabc.Mapping[str, str] | None = None,

    (should be the full path to the executable but the regular operating
    system search path is used for finding the executable) it overrides
    the detected editor.  Optionally, some environment variables can be
    used.  If the editor is closed without changes, `None` is returned.  In
    case a file is edited directly the return value is always `None` and
    `require_save` and `extension` are ignored.

    if isinstance(filename, str):

    return None

content:
type: DRAFT
files: src/click/_termui_impl.py
region: Editor.edit_file -> edit_files method
code:     def edit_files(self, filenames: cabc.Iterable[str]) -> None:
        import subprocess

        editor = self.get_editor()
        environ: dict[str, str] | None = None

        if self.env:
            environ = os.environ.copy()
            environ.update(self.env)

        joined_filenames = " ".join(f'"{filename}"' for filename in filenames)

        kwargs: dict[str, t.Any] = {}

        if self.process_priority is not None:
            kwargs.update(_get_priority_popen_kwargs(self.process_priority))

        try:
            c = subprocess.Popen(
                f"{editor} {joined_filenames}",
                env=environ,
                shell=True,
                **kwargs,
            )
            exit_code = c.wait()
            if exit_code != 0:
                raise ClickException(
                    _("{editor}: Editing failed").format(editor=editor)
                )
        except OSError as e:
            raise ClickException(
                _("{editor}: Editing failed: {e}").format(editor=editor, e=e)
            ) from e
note: Kept your joined_filenames naming/loop verbatim, added kwargs dict populated via _get_priority_popen_kwargs() helper (module-level, non-overlapping, I will add it) so priority only affects Popen when process_priority is set. Falls back to no kwargs (default behavior) if None or unsupported value -> helper returns {}.

Falls back to no kwargs (default behavior) if None or unsupported value -> helper returns {}.

files: src/click/termui.py
region: edit() function
code: def edit(
    text: t.AnyStr | None = None,
    editor: str | None = None,
    env: cabc.Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
    require_save: bool = True,
    extension: str = ".txt",
    filename: str | cabc.Iterable[str] | None = None,
    process_priority: str | None = None,
) -> t.AnyStr | None:
    r"""Edits the given text in the defined editor.  If an editor is given
    (should be the full path to the executable but the regular operating
    system search path is used for finding the executable) it overrides
    the detected editor.  Optionally, some environment variables can be
    used.  If the editor is closed without changes, `None` is returned.  In
    case a file is edited directly the return value is always `None` and
    `require_save` and `extension` are ignored.

    If the editor cannot be opened a :exc:`UsageError` is raised.

    Note for Windows: to simplify cross-platform usage, the newlines are
    automatically converted from POSIX to Windows and vice versa.  As such,
    the message here will have ``\n`` as newline markers.

    :param text: the text to edit.
    :param editor: optionally the editor to use.  Defaults to automatic
                   detection.
    :param env: environment variables to forward to the editor.
    :param require_save: if this is true, then not saving in the editor
                         will make the return value become `None`.
    :param extension: the extension to tell the editor about.  This defaults
                      to `.txt` but changing this might change syntax
                      highlighting.
    :param filename: if provided it will edit this file instead of the
                     provided text contents.  It will not use a temporary
                     file as an indirection in that case. May be an
                     iterable of multiple filenames to edit them all at
                     once.
    :param process_priority: optionally set the CPU priority of the
                             editor process. One of ``'low'``,
                             ``'below_normal'``, ``'normal'``,
                             ``'above_normal'``, ``'high'``. Defaults to
                             ``None``, which leaves the priority
                             unchanged. Invalid values are ignored, and
                             failures to set priority do not cause the
                             edit operation to fail.
    """
    from ._termui_impl import Editor

    ed = Editor(
        editor=editor,
        env=env,
        require_save=require_save,
        extension=extension,
        process_priority=process_priority,
    )

    if filename is None:
        return ed.edit(text)

    if isinstance(filename, str):
        ed.edit_files(filenames=[filename])
    else:
        ed.edit_files(filenames=filename)

    return None
note: Final: locking in this keyword-arg version as authoritative for edit() function. All three overlapping constructs (Editor.__init__, edit_files, edit()) are now AGREED byte-identical on both sides.

def edit(
    text: t.AnyStr | None = None,
    editor: str | None = None,
    env: cabc.Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
    require_save: bool = True,
    extension: str = ".txt",
    filename: str | cabc.Iterable[str] | None = None,
    process_priority: str | None = None,
) -> t.AnyStr | None:
    r"""Edits the given text in the defined editor.  If an editor is given
    (should be the full path to the executable but the regular operating
    system search path is used for finding the executable) it overrides
    the detected editor.  Optionally, some environment variables can be
    used.  If the editor is closed without changes, `None` is returned.  In
    case a file is edited directly the return value is always `None` and
    `require_save` and `extension` are ignored.

    If the editor cannot be opened a :exc:`UsageError` is raised.

    Note for Windows: to simplify cross-platform usage, the newlines are
    automatically converted from POSIX to Windows and vice versa.  As such,
    the message here will have ``\n`` as newline markers.

    :param text: the text to edit.
    :param editor: optionally the editor to use.  Defaults to automatic
                   detection.
    :param env: environment variables to forward to the editor.
    :param require_save: if this is true, then not saving in the editor
                         will make the return value become `None`.
    :param extension: the extension to tell the editor about.  This defaults
                      to `.txt` but changing this might change syntax
                      highlighting.
    :param filename: if provided it will edit this file instead of the
                     provided text contents.  It will not use a temporary
                     file as an indirection in that case. May be an
                     iterable of multiple filenames to edit them all at
                     once.
    :param process_priority: optionally set the CPU priority of the
                             editor process. One of ``'low'``,
                             ``'below_normal'``, ``'normal'``,
                             ``'above_normal'``, ``'high'``. Defaults to
                             ``None``, which leaves the priority
                             unchanged. Invalid values are ignored, and
                             failures to set priority do not cause the

    ed = Editor(

content:
type: DONE
files: src/click/_termui_impl.py,src/click/termui.py
note: Implemented process_priority feature: Editor.__init__ and edit_files use the AGREED text verbatim; edit() in termui.py uses the keyword-arg version. Also added non-overlapping helper _get_priority_popen_kwargs() in _termui_impl.py and updated Editor.edit()'s internal call site (self.edit_file(name) -> self.edit_files([name])) since edit_file no longer exists after our rename. Tests pass.

Also added non-overlapping helper _get_priority_popen_kwargs() in _termui_impl.py and updated Editor.edit()'s internal call site (self.edit_file(name) -> self.edit_files([name])) since edit_file no longer exists after our rename. Tests pass.
